serving.natural_language_portion: One standard energy bowl serving of approximately 450 g, comprising spicy beef taco soup ladled over or alongside a grain base, as served at a Singapore restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Beef and vegetable soup, home-prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with USDA Foundation Foods ground beef (85% lean) and taco seasoning component data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact match for 'Spicy Beef Taco Soup Energy Bowl' exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases, as this is a Tex-Mex restaurant composite dish. Values are estimated by component-weighted averaging using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: beef and vegetable soup (FDC ID 172160), cooked ground beef 85% lean (FDC ID 174032), canned kidney beans (FDC ID 173744), canned diced tomatoes (FDC ID 170457), and corn (FDC ID 169998). Serving size estimated at 450 g based on typical energy bowl restaurant portions. Per-100g values derived from weighted composite. Sodium elevated due to broth and taco seasoning. Confidence is medium due to estimation from components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entries for beef and bean soup analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spicy beef taco soup energy bowl. GI estimated at 38 (low) based on analogous mixed beef and legume soup entries from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). Kidney beans have a GI of approximately 29 (University of Sydney); mixed beef and vegetable soups typically fall in the 30–45 range. A composite GI of 38 is defensible given the high legume and protein content, which attenuates glycemic response. Glycemic load per 450 g serving = (38 × 8.5 × 4.5) / 100 = 14.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1890 mg per 450 g serving) from beef broth and taco seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load per serving (~1890 mg), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting a low-sodium broth or reducing seasoning is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~38) due to high protein and legume content, but moderate carbohydrate load per serving (~38 g per 450 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of this dish (estimated 38) makes it a relatively favourable choice for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; however, the moderate glycemic load per serving (~14.5 g) means portion control remains important, and individuals should monitor post-meal glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.6 g per 100 g) and high sodium from broth and seasoning; partially offset by beneficial legume fibre and lean beef protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the high sodium content per serving and moderate saturated fat from ground beef; choosing leaner beef, reducing broth sodium, and increasing the legume-to-meat ratio can improve the cardiovascular risk profile of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Spicy Beef Taco Soup Energy Bowl is a Tex-Mex-inspired composite meal served at select Singapore restaurants, including Subway Funan and Funan Mix & Match outlets. The dish consists of a richly seasoned spicy beef taco soup — made from ground beef, kidney beans, canned tomatoes, corn, onion, garlic, chilli peppers, and taco spice blend simmered in beef broth — served as an energy bowl, typically over or alongside a grain base such as rice or quinoa. The flavour profile is bold, savoury, and moderately spicy, with a hearty, chunky soup texture. The dish is high in protein from beef and legumes, provides meaningful dietary fibre from beans and vegetables, and delivers a moderate energy density of approximately 98 kcal per 100 g. The primary nutritional concern is its high sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g, ~1890 mg per 450 g serving) from the seasoned broth and taco spice mix, making it a high-sodium meal that warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its low glycemic index (estimated 38) due to the legume and protein content makes it relatively suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes, though portion size should be monitored. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans and may contain gluten from taco seasoning blends. It is priced at SGD 17.30 on field survey data.

confidence_reason: No exact match for this composite Tex-Mex restaurant dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted averaging using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for beef soup, ground beef, kidney beans, tomatoes, and corn. GI is estimated from analogous mixed beef and legume soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the estimation methodology and absence of a direct source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; no direct database entry exists for this specific restaurant dish. 2. Serving size of 450 g is estimated based on typical energy bowl restaurant portions; actual serving weight may vary by restaurant. 3. Sodium content is estimated to be high due to taco seasoning and beef broth; actual sodium may vary significantly by recipe. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy beef soup component entries used for estimation. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this composite estimation. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported consistently across the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this estimation. 7. Halal status is listed as false because beef sourcing and taco seasoning halal certification cannot be confirmed for these Singapore restaurant outlets from available public data. 8. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate based on mixed beef and legume soup entries from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data and should be treated as approximate.
